title: Tracking human mobility for wildlife conservation
methods: coupling human and animal movement data
data_sources: human mobility data (class; specific sources not stated)
animal movement / telemetry (class; specific sources not stated)

data_scope.article_type: Science Perspective / commentary (not a primary empirical study)
data_scope.full_text_access: publisher returned 403 Forbidden; article.html and reference list unavailable to this digest

core_conclusion: Large-scale human mobility data can expand ecological analyses of how people affect wild animals: by measuring where and when people actually go, mobility data turn the static human footprint into a dynamic, behaviorally explicit driver of wildlife disturbance, offering a new evidence base for conservation.
why_it_matters: Reframes the human side of human-wildlife conflict by making people as dynamic and measurable as animals; upgrades static human-footprint maps to time-varying activity; bridges the mobility-data and movement-ecology communities; informs protected-area zoning and seasonal management.
why_nature: Cross-disciplinary Science Perspective fusing human-mobility data science with conservation ecology; couples space, human behavior, and a high-consequence problem (biodiversity loss); forward-looking and methodological; broad readership across ecology, GeoAI, and policy.
added_value_to_my_research: Spatiotemporal KDE of human visitation -> time-resolved wildlife disturbance field
Two-layer human-animal movement graph -> physics-informed graph operator for co-occurrence
R2SFCA recast for refugia supply vs human-pressure demand in conservation zoning
Explainable ML / MGWR to attribute which human-movement features drive disturbance
Multi-source fusion (phone + social media + remote sensing) into a FAIR conservation data layer
